sentences.text: Well, I think that it was obvious that something is going to take place.
Not necessarily ominous, but something very different.
I think that we had started to have some refugees from Austria.
And when I was 18 years old, I had a surgery.
And I was in a hospital in-- for a few days.
And I met with a gentleman, with an Austrian refugee.
And my mother actually helped him to get-- renew the visa.
So it was the Jewish people who escaped.
I don't recall any things that we knew, actually-- the real danger, what real means for the Jewish people.
But we certainly did know that the Jewish people severely discriminated against.
And it's-- if they run away, it has to be something very serious that they leave everything behind.
But of course, they didn't even know what they're running away from.
But I just felt that the circumstances were so desperate.
The interesting thing is that things changed around us very rapidly.
I think that I mentioned previously this Hitler.
And I think this is a-- to me-- a very important issue.
Because usually , people when the word, Hitler, come, and I tried to talk about it, they try to say, this was a madman and that's about it.
I think that would be a great underestimation of his importance and his abilities.
I think he was definitely a genius.
What he used his genius for is another issue.
I think he changed-- he had, first of all, such a charismatic speech that he was able to arise a lot of enthusiasm.
And he had influenced the public in such an extensive way.
I don't know you know, or not, but the Volkswagen was not the first thing Hitler started with-- Volksradio was the first thing.
He wanted the radio in every one home because he was able to influence people through his speech.
My brother, who was a Jewish student in Switzerland said that when he heard Hitler speaking, he couldn't be quiet.
He was so enthused until he read what the words he was saying.
And he didn't say anything.
He was just an incredible charismatic speaker.
I think that if you look at what Hitler did for the world, as far as the present is concerned, our current election is a very good example.
You have to appear in the crowd by television and other mean.
It's a salesmanship.
And he was a terrific salesman.
He was able to mobilize a very civilized country for his purpose and for his intent, which eventually was a very destructive one.
I think we have to understand that concept, otherwise everybody think, how come this could happen?
I think he was an incredible charismatic person who, from the misery of Germany, he kind of remade the Germans very proud and very prosperous.
And then eventually introducing this very horrifying dogma, which he was obsessed with.
Which was number one is the hate of anything what Jews did.
It's not was only hating about the capitalism, the bank, but he was hating the communism.
He was hating even the Bible, itself.
So it was an obsession, which eventually led to this destruction of these many, many million people.
No, he was known.
The only thing is the human principles was not-- they was against anything because it was an absolutely discriminatory comments, you know.
Separating certain people as inferior.
And that's not according to the Bible.
In Hungarian, of course.
Yes.
I was-- you know, and that was our hope was that on the way, we're eventually going to be free or equal person if the war is lost by the Germans.
And so that we desperately-- I spent daily, hours and hours to listen, you know, what's happening.
And unfortunately, it was everything went sour from the long, long time.
Yes, the Hungarian government actually was aliens of the Germans.
And it was the so-called Axis-- Axis meant the German and Italian relationship.
And the Hungarians was part of that.
Hungarians was participating in a certain way, even in the war against the Russians.
And even declared war against the United States, which was absolutely nonsensical.

I think it was a joke in Hungary that the Hungarian governor was-- actually, the joke was that Roosevelt asked when they received the declaration of the Hungarian war against America said, what's happening here?
Who is the person who declared this?
He said, where is a Navy officer?
And where is the Sea of Hungary?
We don't have any sea.
And why did they, he said, they create a war against us?
Do we have any border?
No, we doesn't have any border.
Who is against?
It's not against a neighbor, but they created a war against the United States.
So it didn't make any sense.
So the Hungarians were on the side of the Germans.
And they sent troops against the Russians.
And-- The Hungarians sent troops.
The Hungarians sent the troops.
And then that time, they started to send the Jewish boys with their troops as a working and a forced labor.
And then-- then we started to understand that this something is very, very serious things happening.
I think that-- I don't know that it arised or not.
It became more obvious, because the Nazis-- the Nazi-oriented people became more and more louder and louder.
So they had little stickers and put it on different places.
They had it on newspaper, and so forth.
So it became obvious that this is a-- I would say maybe the voice became louder.
I think anti-Semitism is exists-- existed and still exists almost anywhere in east Europe, and probably many other places.
I don't think it's anything new.
But it became more voice to it.
You were in the gymnasium at this time.
I'm in the gymnasium at this time.
And as I mentioned to you, it became even from outside the window, it became obvious that there's a change taking place.
I would assume that this became for some more obvious, because they probably heard it at home.
I don't know that how many of them were, or how many of them were not.
I think that it became more obvious.
No, no, no.
Not whatsoever.
And many of my teachers are very much were even, indeed, teaching appropriate behavior and would not go for it, but some did.
So Hungary took in Jews who were escaping from other countries, and yet it was a--
Right-- well, the Hungarians were in a very peculiar situation.
But they-- they allowed for-- example, as I mentioned to you, the Polish army to go through Hungary.
And they had a couple of refugees in Hungary from Poland, as well as from Austria.
They get temporary visa.
It was obviously harder and harder to renew, but it was some there, yes.
And the Hungarians were, in a sense, to some people they were neutral.
Yet they were not.
No, no.
They were not neutral by any means.
Right.
Well I, first of all, I was at that time stayed in high school so I didn't think.
And it was no time to make plans in that situation.
[LAUGHS] You live by day by day.
You don't have any plan in the mind.
Some did, some didn't.
And if a German teacher was for it, then the French teacher was against it.
So you know, [LAUGHS] it's-- you cannot make a generalization.
I don't think we can make generalization on anything.
The thing is, at that point, I was stopped, because it was no time for me.
My parents could not send me out to study anywhere.
They couldn't even afford it at that point.
My brother actually cost them a lot of money, and it was very difficult.
You can imagine a Hungarian person, illegal, sending off money to foreign countries to educate a child, because at the beginning it was legal.
But eventually, my mother had to find a different way to reap the amounts to send food and money for him to maintain him.
But it was no possibility for me that time to go.
And so, at that time, I was just taking certain courses such as typewriting.
I took some art courses.
And-- and I was just hoping that the time of the end of the war will take place-- but it didn't.
Well, I have an uncle who is a very well-known Hungarian painter.
His name is Mardjor, which is Hungarian Mannheimer, from Mannheim-- Gustav.
And he is a-- and I was just always liked to draw.
So then-- then after then, I had an opportunity to go to evening classes and take some art classes, until the German came in.
The German occupied Hungary in March 19, 1944.
This is a very interesting thing, because how you do occupy someone who is on your side?
And the reason for that is because Romanians already made a peace with the Russians, and so on.
So the German tried to protect that Hungarians could not do that-- the Hungarian government shouldn't do it, in order to maintain their location.
Hungary has a very important geographical location, regarding east, and west, and south, and north.
So that it was militarily very important, so they occupied Budapest in March 19th, 1944.
And from there on, our life really changed.
That was because of its proximity to the Soviet Union?
Yes.
And Russian and the German troops were still in--
Right.
You know, in 1944 in March, the Americans were already in Sicily.
The Russian already beat the German in Stalingrad and pushed the German back, at least during the winter offensive.
And so that the things became very different.
It was very obvious that Africa was, of course, completely lost for them.
And it was very obvious they are going lose the war.
Yes.
I think the Hungarian government probably indeed tried to make some kind of contact.
I think very foolishly, because they didn't do it the right way, and so were late.
And they couldn't do it.
Well, when the German came in, within a matter of a week, is we had to be identified with a big yellow star, which was a 10-centimeter star.
Which is about 4 and 1/2 inches at least-- on our coat, or jacket, or shirt if we went outside.
That was the first step to do.
And then I went to a-- a-- I was-- I don't know what to do.
And the Jewish organization was established, which was in the center of the Jewish religious center of Budapest.
And then it was an organization which is-- had some kind of liaison between the Germans and the Jews.
And the Jews thought, so naively, that by negotiating with the Germans they can able to buy time, or something.
As a matter of fact, it was-- I understand Eichmann was who sent out some-- two Jewish peasant to Turkey to make some kind of agreement to divest in Jewish leaders in order to buy the Hungarians.
For example, $400 or something.
But they requested trucks, or something like that.
This--my understanding, these two Hungarians were arrested.
And they never had any success, because they considered them some kind of German agent or something.
And so that-- which it's very interesting, as far as, you know, responsibility viewpoint, because it would be possibility to save many people, if they would make that connection.
But they didn't.
And I can understand why.
But on the other hand, you know, when we're talking about responsibilities, this could be a very interesting issue to discuss.
So I worked through this place.
And I think probably in you could see this kind of-- and Schindler goes to these very crowded rooms.
That kind of organization I was working.
And I was working in the so-called Contra Division.
And Contra Division mean that-- in Budapest, which is obviously the center, and then the country people who is outside.
And one day, a very apprehensive man came.
And wanted to talk with the leader of the Hungarian-- of the Contra Division.
And somehow, his behavior was so peculiar that I wanted to hear what he had to say.
So instead of leaving the room, I stayed inside in the room.
And he described the first time that, for the Hungarians, eventually had to-- Hungarian Jews had to go in a ghetto.
They had to be taken away their belonging.
And then in the country, within a matter of weeks after the Germans came in, they were put next to the railroad station.
Put in the box cars, crowdedly.
And I think, [INAUDIBLE] if they had their clothes on.
And without any kind of supply, they were crammed together in these box car used for cows and cattle.
And they were blocked, and they were going away to an unknown destiny.
When I heard this, this really changed my whole concept what's happening here.
First of all, I became aware that by the circumstances anything like this happening, then the outcome, the future goal to do with these people is not anymore about killing them.
That was the first time I became personally aware of this.
Or not only just informed, because we were not informed.
So then, from there on, whole-- my concept of the future completely changed.
For example, I was very scared from air raids, which did started to happening since the German came to Budapest-- or into the country.
And then from there on, I realized that every drop of bomb is going to fall is going to lead to my liberation.
So I did not only have freedom.
I was euphoric with each sound when they came and bombed the Germans and us.
When you were in the Jewish organization, were you able to do anything for the people in the country?
No, no.
No.
I was just a very unimportant person.
Probably opening doors or taking papers from one room to the other.
But that didn't last too long.
Because then, very soon after, every Hungarian Jewish man was-- had to relieve-- who was between 18 to 48 years old in June, to a so-called forced labor.
And so that's where I got to.
Well, I think that it was-- I never forget the day.
Before I was taken away, which was in June 3rd, on 1944, I had to go and report to a certain place.
[NON-ENGLISH]. often one of my friend, who lived a floor above me, and his father went down and we saw my father.
And I looked out through the window, and I see them two walk.
And I said to my father, they probably never see each other.
And then the next day, I had to go.
And he came and helped me to go to the city car.
Yes.
And I had to pack, you know, the certain things which are on your back, or backpack, or something.
And you take some essentials with you.
Then we went to the corner, and I went on the city car.
And we know we may not see each other, but we couldn't say goodbye.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] That was the last time.
I'm sorry.
Then I went with 280 men to a working camp.
And we were assigned to the Hungarian bridge builder units.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that was in a place known [NON-ENGLISH], which is a city now between Slovakia and Budapest.
It's actually two parts-- but at that time, it was belonged to Hungary only.
And we were working there.
And it was a very hard physical labor.
We were well fed and very cruelly handled.
We were not in any way out of danger.
And I was, of course, never in my life worked anything physically.
But you were young and you adopt yourself to it.
And they were very cruel with us.
For example, they took away from the pictures of the family members, and tear it apart that you're not going to see your family, so--
You don't need these pictures anymore.
Oh, yes.
We are all Jewish.
We were assigned to the military unit to build bridges.
But it was more or less just a local physical work what we had to do.
Hungarian military, yes.
The-- the-- I think the name of the Hungarian officer who was responsible for us was named Kardos-- K-A-R-D-O-S.
And it was a few others.
But we had-- he had a special group of individuals who worked.
In other words, supervised us.
And when he left, every weekend he went to Budapest.
And they just did a different kind of way to make our life miserable.
Well, they, for example, they searched us all over.
And they take up anything away from us.
And if someone did not do something or which they thought appropriate, they will hold them up and they hang them up to this way.
So their feet is actually didn't feel-- touch the floor or the ground, except in the landing.
So it was this kind of management.
Well, the officers had very little to do with us, very frankly.
And then they always assigned us to some kind of soldiers.
And they handled us.
And that was different.
You know, some were worse than the other.
We had to dig out some un-exploded bombs and things like that nature.
Or when was an air raid, we were forced to go next to the place when they stored their bombs and explosives.
So as I said, if it is hit, then it wouldn't be wasted.
Yeah, right.
No.
Yes, I--we received mail in a limited fashion, and Anna-- this wonderful person, one day, sent me a hat.
I'll never forgot that.
And she said that, I send you a very expensive hat.
And I thought so, that's not her style.
She would never say that she sent me something which cost a lot of money.
So I figured out it was money inside in the hat.
[LAUGHS] And so I opened and it was indeed money inside so that I could buy something for it.
Did you ever go home?
No.
We never could go home.
And actually, at one point in 1945, for a few weeks we were moved backwards.
In other words, eastwards in a place called, [NON-ENGLISH].
And we worked there in a refinery, which is using gasolines.
And Anna came to see me.
And it was such a beautiful experience for me that this poor soul came and brought me some food.
And she was the very last who went back to Budapest hanging on the side of the train.
And she was a crippled person.
It was so very, very beautiful.
She had a bone abnormalities.
Tuberculosis of the bone.
It's hard for me to say.
We had to work from morning to the late evening.
I think people probably could pray.
I don't remember, very frankly, I did or not.
But we had a prayer book with us.
I think in the morning or in the evening you could pray.
But it was nothing special-- pro or contra.
Oh, we were together, period.
I mean, we were in the barracks.
And you could pray together.
If someone wanted to pray together.
I don't remember I did.
Next what happened is that the Russians now occupying Budapest.
And we had, in January, 1945, we had to force walk to the border of Austria or Germany.
And in there, when we get there, this time now you meet with other group of people.
This is the start of the beginning of the so-called forced walk.
And we went to a place Sopron, S-O-P-R-O-N.
And I saw something very unusual.
And that was a coach with an open end.
And the hang was out.
And the old country roads, it was bouncing like a ball.
And I thought it was a dead body.
And eventually we were taken to a brick burner, which is edge of the village.
And they threw this body down.
And for my biggest surprise, this was a living human being who could not put his head up.
He's crawling like a worm.
And it was a skeleton.
Then eventually, we were forced to go to this brick burner, which was full with hundreds of dying and dead people.
And the smell was indescribable-- it just was unbelievable.
It was a dead body under excrement and in ditches and so on.
And we got invaded by a million of the lice, which eventually started the typhoid fever.
And that was in Sopron, right.
In 1945 January.
Yes.
And then we were forced to go inside.
And then when we were forced to go inside, you can imagine in a very dark circumstances, millions of lice start to invade you.
Because that's what these people are dying from.
From infection of the-- transmitted by the lice.
But we didn't know that.
Starvation-- oh, yeah.
We're around and they were all starving.
I mean, they were skeletons.
They couldn't even walk.
No, it wasn't a concentration camp.
It was just a camp like collected people to send it further.
But I don't know exactly what was the basic name why-- what was going on there before.
Yes.
And then my suspicion was confirmed that we're going to be killed.
Mostly were Jews, yes.
No, no.
It was no burning.
No, no.
We didn't have to do anything.
We were just only two days.
Next, they took us on a box train and they took us to Germany.
Well, it was not too comfortable.
But we didn't stay there inside too long.
Because when we get to Germany, we were actually on the border.
And it was only one day kind of trip or even less than that.
And overnight.
And then the SS took us out.
And so we arrived in Austria.
OK.
Good.
